name: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 16.02.2026

origin.filename: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 16.02.2026.pdf

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Key Insights
texts.text: Key Insights

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Rabi Sowing Status: Summer sowing data collection has been started from 9 th  February, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary stage. As on February 16, 2026, the area sown under summer crops is 15.18 lakh ha as compared to 14.75 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  Of this, area of 12.80 lakh ha has been sown under rice this year.
texts.text: Rabi Sowing Status: Summer sowing data collection has been started from 9 th  February, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary stage. As on February 16, 2026, the area sown under summer crops is 15.18 lakh ha as compared to 14.75 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  Of this, area of 12.80 lakh ha has been sown under rice this year.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  As on 13.02.2026, 99% of area has been harvested for Kharif crops all over India.
texts.text:  As on 13.02.2026, 99% of area has been harvested for Kharif crops all over India.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Weather Update :
texts.text: 2. Weather Update :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a) Temperature: Minimum temperatures  are likely to  be  below  normal  in  East  India,  East M.P., Vidarbha, Marathwada, East U.P., some parts of NE India and many parts of South India. Minimum temperatures is likely to be above normal in many parts of NW India, Gujarat, Madhya Maharashtra and some parts of South Karnataka.
texts.text: Temperature: Minimum temperatures  are likely to  be  below  normal  in  East  India,  East M.P., Vidarbha, Marathwada, East U.P., some parts of NE India and many parts of South India. Minimum temperatures is likely to be above normal in many parts of NW India, Gujarat, Madhya Maharashtra and some parts of South Karnataka.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: b) Rainfall Distribution: From January 01- February 15 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is  52%  lower  than  its  Long  Period  Average  (LPA),  with  regional  variations:  (i)  East  & Northeast  India:  (-)  90%;  (ii)  Northwest  India:  (-)  38%;  (iii)  Central  India:  (-)  84%;  and South Peninsula: (-) 25%.
texts.text: Rainfall Distribution: From January 01- February 15 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is  52%  lower  than  its  Long  Period  Average  (LPA),  with  regional  variations:  (i)  East  & Northeast  India:  (-)  90%;  (ii)  Northwest  India:  (-)  38%;  (iii)  Central  India:  (-)  84%;  and South Peninsula: (-) 25%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Isolated  rainfall/snowfall  likely  over  Jammu  -  Kashmir  -  Ladakh  -  Gilgit  -  Baltistan  Muzaffarabad during 17 th  -19 th  and over Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand on 17 th  & 18 th  February.
texts.text:  Isolated  rainfall/snowfall  likely  over  Jammu  -  Kashmir  -  Ladakh  -  Gilgit  -  Baltistan  Muzaffarabad during 17 th  -19 th  and over Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand on 17 th  & 18 th  February.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Isolated rainfall with thunderstorm, lightning likely over Punjab, Haryana, Chandigarh, Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan on 17 th  & 18 th ; over Madhya Pradesh on 18 th  & 19 th  with thunderstorm, lightning likely over Nicobar Islands on 16 th  & 17 th  and over Andaman & Nicobar Islands on 18 th  & 19 th  February.
texts.text:  Isolated rainfall with thunderstorm, lightning likely over Punjab, Haryana, Chandigarh, Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan on 17 th  & 18 th ; over Madhya Pradesh on 18 th  & 19 th  with thunderstorm, lightning likely over Nicobar Islands on 16 th  & 17 th  and over Andaman & Nicobar Islands on 18 th  & 19 th  February.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Above normal rainfall is likely in Andaman-Nicobar islands, Rainfall is also likely Tamil Nadu, J&K, some parts of Himachal Pradesh, some parts of Uttarakhand and some parts of Arunachal Pradesh.
texts.text:  Above normal rainfall is likely in Andaman-Nicobar islands, Rainfall is also likely Tamil Nadu, J&K, some parts of Himachal Pradesh, some parts of Uttarakhand and some parts of Arunachal Pradesh.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Gradual  rise  in  minimum  temperatures  by  2-3°C  during  next  2  days  &  fall  by  2-3  °C thereafter likely over Northwest India. No significant change in minimum temperatures likely over rest parts of the country.
texts.text:  Gradual  rise  in  minimum  temperatures  by  2-3°C  during  next  2  days  &  fall  by  2-3  °C thereafter likely over Northwest India. No significant change in minimum temperatures likely over rest parts of the country.

texts.label: page_footer
texts.orig: Agricultural Statistics Division| Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare |Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare
texts.text: Agricultural Statistics Division| Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare |Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Inputs Situation
texts.text: 3. Inputs Situation

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a) Water  Reservoir  Status :  As  on  12  February  2026,  the  total  live  water  storage  in  166 important  reservoirs  across  the  country  (monitored  by  CWC)  is  118.140  billion  cubic meter,  which  is  64%  of  total  live  storage  capacity  (183.565  BCM).  The  current  year's storage is nearly 110.29 percent of last year's storage and 124.97 percent of the average storage of the last ten years during the corresponding period. The overall storage position is better than the corresponding period of last year in the country as a whole and is also better than the Normal storage during the corresponding period.
texts.text: Water  Reservoir  Status :  As  on  12  February  2026,  the  total  live  water  storage  in  166 important  reservoirs  across  the  country  (monitored  by  CWC)  is  118.140  billion  cubic meter,  which  is  64%  of  total  live  storage  capacity  (183.565  BCM).  The  current  year's storage is nearly 110.29 percent of last year's storage and 124.97 percent of the average storage of the last ten years during the corresponding period. The overall storage position is better than the corresponding period of last year in the country as a whole and is also better than the Normal storage during the corresponding period.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: b) Groundwater Levels :  In  August  2025,  In  Post-monsoon 2025, around 90% of the country's monitoring stations exhibited a depth to water level within 10 meters below ground level. Deeper  water  levels  of  more  than  20  m  are  observed  in  around  5%  of  stations  of  the country  covering  mainly  the  western  states,  especially  Western  Rajasthan,  Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh.
texts.text: Groundwater Levels :  In  August  2025,  In  Post-monsoon 2025, around 90% of the country's monitoring stations exhibited a depth to water level within 10 meters below ground level. Deeper  water  levels  of  more  than  20  m  are  observed  in  around  5%  of  stations  of  the country  covering  mainly  the  western  states,  especially  Western  Rajasthan,  Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh  during  Post-monsoon  2025  has  been  significantly  influenced  by  the  over extraction of groundwater compared to the recharge during the monsoon period.
texts.text:  The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh  during  Post-monsoon  2025  has  been  significantly  influenced  by  the  over extraction of groundwater compared to the recharge during the monsoon period.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Annual  water  level  comparison  with  the  previous  year  Post-monsoon  2024  to  Postmonsoon  2025  has  shown  that  about  63%  of  total  analyzed  stations  of  the  country experienced rise in  annual  water level fluctuation may  be  attributed to the less groundwater extraction compared to 2024.
texts.text:  Annual  water  level  comparison  with  the  previous  year  Post-monsoon  2024  to  Postmonsoon  2025  has  shown  that  about  63%  of  total  analyzed  stations  of  the  country experienced rise in  annual  water level fluctuation may  be  attributed to the less groundwater extraction compared to 2024.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  About 73% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level  in  the  decadal  mean  water  level  fluctuation  of  2015-2024  with  respect  to  Postmonsoon 2025.
texts.text:  About 73% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level  in  the  decadal  mean  water  level  fluctuation  of  2015-2024  with  respect  to  Postmonsoon 2025.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The  Groundwater  Drought  Indices  indicate  that  in Post-monsoon  2025 ,  groundwater conditions  across  India  remained  largely  stable,  with 91%  of  monitoring  stations showing no  deficit .  However,  localized  deficits  were  observed  in  several  regions  notably in Meghalaya, Nagaland, and the Dadar Nagar Havili & Daman & Diu.
texts.text:  The  Groundwater  Drought  Indices  indicate  that  in Post-monsoon  2025 ,  groundwater conditions  across  India  remained  largely  stable,  with 91%  of  monitoring  stations showing no  deficit .  However,  localized  deficits  were  observed  in  several  regions  notably in Meghalaya, Nagaland, and the Dadar Nagar Havili & Daman & Diu.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: c) Fertilizers : The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products.
texts.text: Fertilizers : The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products.

texts.text: Agricultural Statistics Division| Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare |Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Pests  &  Diseases :  There  is  no  major  pest  incidence  that  has  been  reported.  The  overall intensity of pests and diseases remained below the Economic Threshold Level.
texts.text: Pests  &  Diseases :  There  is  no  major  pest  incidence  that  has  been  reported.  The  overall intensity of pests and diseases remained below the Economic Threshold Level.

texts.text:  During the week (05 Feb - 11 Feb 2026), lower rainfall was observed, as compared to Long Period Average (LPA) by 94%. Cumulative seasonal rainfall (01 st Jan to 11 Feb 2026) is lower than LPA by 45%.

texts.text: Overall,  rainfall  is  likely  to  be  below  normal  over  most  parts  of  the  country,  except  the southern peninsular India, where it is likely to be near normal to above normal .
